hero_sub: Habits are the compound interest of self-improvement. Learn the science of how they form and the practical strategies to design a life by intention, not by default.
definition: A habit is a routine or behavior that is performed regularly and, in many cases, automatically. It is the brain's way of conserving energy by turning a sequence of actions into an automatic routine. The process of habit formation can be broken down into a simple four-step loop: cue, craving, response, and reward.

why_cards.title: Compound Growth
why_cards.desc: Small improvements of 1% each day lead to massive results over time. Habits are the vehicle for long-term transformation.

why_cards.title: Energy Efficiency
why_cards.desc: By automating routine tasks, habits free up cognitive energy for more complex decision-making and creative work.

why_cards.title: Identity Shifting
why_cards.desc: Every action you take is a vote for the type of person you wish to become. Your habits are how you embody your identity.

why_cards.title: Consistency
why_cards.desc: Habits provide a reliable structure that ensures progress even on days when motivation is low.

pillars.title: The Habit Loop
pillars.desc: Understand the mechanics of Cue (trigger), Craving (motivation), Response (action), and Reward (satisfaction).

pillars.title: Environment Design
pillars.desc: Make the cues of good habits obvious and the cues of bad habits invisible. Your environment shapes your behavior.

pillars.title: Identity-Based Habits
pillars.desc: Focus on who you want to become, not what you want to achieve. The goal is not to run a marathon, but to become a runner.

case_studies.name: British Cycling — Marginal Gains
case_studies.desc: Dave Brailsford transformed British cycling by looking for 1% improvements in everything—from bike seats to pillows. These tiny improvements compounded into Olympic dominance.
case_studies.lesson: You don't need radical changes to see radical results. Small, consistent improvements in your daily habits are more powerful than one-off transformations.

case_studies.name: The 'Paper Clip' Strategy
case_studies.desc: A young stockbroker moved 120 paper clips from one jar to another every time he made a sales call. This visual progress cue made the habit of calling addictive and led to his massive success.
case_studies.lesson: Visual cues and tracking provide immediate feedback and satisfaction, which are crucial for reinforcing the habit loop.

lessons.title: Make it obvious.
lessons.desc: Use implementation intentions: 'I will [BEHAVIOR] at [TIME] in [LOCATION].'

lessons.title: Make it attractive.
lessons.desc: Use temptation bundling: Pair an action you want to do with an action you need to do.

lessons.title: Make it easy.
lessons.desc: Use the Two-Minute Rule: When you start a new habit, it should take less than two minutes to do.

lessons.title: Make it satisfying.
lessons.desc: Give yourself an immediate reward when you complete your habit to reinforce the loop.

lessons.title: Invert the laws for bad habits.
lessons.desc: To break a bad habit: Make it invisible, unattractive, difficult, and unsatisfying.

lessons.title: Never miss twice.
lessons.desc: Missing once is an accident. Missing twice is the start of a new habit. Get back on track immediately.

lessons.title: Focus on systems, not goals.
lessons.desc: Goals are about the results you want to achieve. Systems are about the processes that lead to those results.

lessons.title: Design your environment.
lessons.desc: Be the architect of your surroundings. Prime your environment for future use.

lessons.title: The Goldilocks Rule.
lessons.desc: Maintain motivation by working on tasks of 'just right' difficulty—not too easy, not too hard.

lessons.title: Habit Stacking.
lessons.desc: Identify a current habit you already have and then stack your new behavior on top of it.

power_line: You do not rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems.
sources: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones.
Duhigg, C. (2012). The Power of Habit: Why We Do What We Do in Life and Business.
Fogg, B.J. (2019). Tiny Habits: The Small Changes That Change Everything.
Rubin, G. (2015). Better Than Before: Mastering the Habits of Our Everyday Lives.
